title: Plasma Cell Disorders
authors: Nikhil C. Munshi, Dan L. Longo, Kenneth C. Anderson

items.title: Understanding Plasma Cell Disorder Development
items.content: Plasma cell disorders arise from monoclonal neoplasms of common progenitors in the late B-lymphocyte lineage. The loss of control over plasma cell maturation and proliferation, which normally occurs upon antigen exposure, leads to these disorders.
keyPoints: Plasma cell disorders include multiple myeloma, Waldenström's macroglobulinemia, primary amyloidosis, and heavy chain diseases.
These conditions are also known as monoclonal gammopathies, paraproteinemias, plasma cell dyscrasias, and dysproteinemias.
Normal B lymphocyte maturation is disrupted in these disorders.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · Plasma Cell Disorders (part 1)

items.title: Classification of Immunoglobulin Variations
items.content: Immunoglobulins are classified based on structural variations into three categories: isotypes, allotypes, and idiotypes. Isotypic determinants distinguish main antibody classes within a species.
keyPoints: Isotypes differentiate among the five heavy chain types (M, G, A, D, E) and two light chain types ($ \kappa $, $ \lambda $).
Isotypic determinants are recognized by heterologous sera but not homologous.
Idiotypes are unique to molecules from a specific clone of antibody-producing cells.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · Plasma Cell Disorders (part 1)

items.title: Antibody Molecular Structure and Function
items.content: Antibodies consist of two heavy chains (~50,000 mol wt) and two light chains (~25,000 mol wt), linked by disulfide bonds. The variable regions form the antigen recognition site.
keyPoints: Each antibody molecule has a constant portion and a variable region with extensive sequence variability.
Variable regions are adjacent due to chain alignment, forming the antigen-binding site.
<10 mg of free light chains are excreted per day under normal conditions.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · Plasma Cell Disorders (part 1)

items.title: Quantification and Significance of M Component in Plasma Cell Tumors
items.content: Electrophoretic analysis identifies an M component, a monoclonal spike in the gamma region, indicative of plasma cell tumors. The monoclonal antibody concentration must be at least 5 g/L (0.5 g/dL) for accurate quantitation, correlating with approximately 10^9 antibody-producing cells.
keyPoints: M component is a sharp spike in the gamma region on electrophoretic analysis.
Monoclonal antibody concentration must be at least 5 g/L (0.5 g/dL) for accurate quantitation.
This concentration corresponds to approximately 10^9 antibody-producing cells.
Electrophoresis provides quantitative assessment of the M component.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · Plasma Cell Disorders (part 2)

items.title: Clinical Implications and Associations of Monoclonal Proteins
items.content: Monoclonal proteins, while useful as tumor markers in plasma cell disorders, are not specific enough for screening asymptomatic patients. They can be detected in various lymphoid and nonlymphoid neoplasms, autoimmune conditions, and even post-transplant immunosuppressed states.
keyPoints: Monoclonal proteins serve as reliable tumor markers but are not specific for screening asymptomatic individuals.
M components may appear in other lymphoid neoplasms like CLL and B- or T-cell lymphomas.
Non-neoplastic conditions such as cirrhosis, sarcoidosis, and autoimmune diseases can also present with M components.
Monoclonal proteins are observed in immunosuppressed patients post-transplant.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · Plasma Cell Disorders (part 2)

items.title: Understanding the Etiology and Genetic Landscape of Multiple Myeloma (MM)
items.content: Multiple myeloma is characterized by malignant proliferation of plasma cells from a single clone, leading to various organ dysfunctions. The etiology involves exposure-related risk factors such as radiation and certain occupational hazards. Genomic studies reveal significant chromosomal alterations and mutations in MM.
keyPoints: MM results from malignant proliferation of plasma cells derived from a single clone.
Risk factors include exposure to nuclear radiation (20-year latency post-World War II) and occupations like farming, woodworking, leatherworking, and petroleum product handling.
Chromosomal alterations: Hyperdiploidy in 50% of patients; translocations involving chromosome 14q32 with partners such as t(11;14)(q13;q32), t(4;14)(p16;q32), and t(14;16).
Common mutations include KRAS (~20%), NRAS (~20%), TP53, DIS3, FAM46C, BRAF (5–10% each); median mutations per transcribed genome ~58, whole genome >7000.
IL-6 may contribute to myeloma cell proliferation. Distinguishing benign from malignant plasma cells is challenging.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY

items.title: Genomic Insights and Mutation Patterns in Multiple Myeloma
items.content: The genomic landscape of multiple myeloma includes a variety of chromosomal alterations and mutations, contributing to its heterogeneity. These insights are crucial for developing targeted therapies.
keyPoints: Recurrent chromosomal alterations include hyperdiploidy (chromosomes 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 15, 19, or 21) and translocations involving chromosome 14q32.
Frequent genetic abnormalities: 13q14 deletion, 1q amplification/deletion, 17p13 deletions.
Median mutations per transcribed genome ~58; whole genome >7000. Heterogeneous mutational landscape with no unifying mutation.
Most frequently mutated genes: KRAS (~20%), NRAS (~20%), TP53, DIS3, FAM46C, BRAF (5–10% each).
Subclonal variants and additional mutations over time indicate genomic evolution driving disease progression.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY

items.title: Incidence of Myeloma by Demographics and Geography
items.content: Myeloma incidence is higher in certain demographics and geographic regions, with notable differences based on age, race, and development level of countries.
keyPoints: In the U.S., 35,780 new myeloma cases were estimated in 2024; median diagnosis age is 69 years.
Males more commonly affected than females; blacks have nearly twice the incidence compared to whites.
Incidence rates per 100,000: 8.1 (white men), 5.1 (white women), 17.1 (black men), 13.0 (black women).
Highest incidence in blacks and Pacific Islanders; lowest in developing countries like Asia.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Pathogenesis of Myeloma: Bone Marrow Microenvironment
items.content: Myeloma cells interact with bone marrow stromal cells and extracellular matrix, promoting growth, survival, drug resistance, and migration.
keyPoints: MM cells bind to BMSCs via adhesion molecules, inducing cytokines like IL-6, IGF-1, VEGF.
Signaling pathways involved: Ras/Raf/MAPK for growth, PI3K/Akt for drug resistance, and PKC for migration.
Interactions with endothelial cells and osteoclasts significantly impact MM cell dynamics.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Manifestations of Myeloma: Bone Pain and Infections
items.content: Bone pain is prevalent in myeloma due to osteoclast activation, while infections are common due to immune dysfunction.
keyPoints: Bone pain affects ~70% of patients; caused by tumor proliferation and increased osteoclast activity.
Common infections include pneumonia (S. pneumoniae) and pyelonephritis (E. coli).
~25% present with recurrent infections, >75% experience serious infections during disease course.
Hypogammaglobulinemia and T-cell dysfunction contribute to increased infection risk.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Renal Dysfunction in Myeloma: Causes and Reversibility
items.content: Renal failure is prevalent in myeloma patients, with nearly 25% experiencing it. Hypercalcemia is the primary cause of renal failure among these patients. Tubular damage from light chain excretion is a common mechanism leading to conditions like adult Fanconi's syndrome. Renal dysfunction due to light chain deposition disease and amyloidosis can be partially reversible with effective therapy.
keyPoints: Renal failure occurs in ~25% of myeloma patients, with some renal pathology noted in >50%.
Hypercalcemia is the most common cause of renal failure in these patients.
Tubular damage from light chain excretion can lead to adult Fanconi's syndrome.
Renal dysfunction due to light chain deposition disease and amyloidosis is partially reversible with effective therapy.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS (part 2) | B

items.title: Anemia in Myeloma: Prevalence and Causes
items.content: Normocytic and normochromic anemia is common in myeloma, affecting ~80% of patients. It results from marrow replacement by tumor cells, inhibition of hematopoiesis, reduced erythropoietin production, and long-term therapy effects. Megaloblastic anemia due to folate or vitamin B12 deficiency can also occur.
keyPoints: Normocytic and normochromic anemia occurs in ~80% of myeloma patients.
Causes include marrow replacement by tumor cells, inhibition of hematopoiesis, reduced erythropoietin production, and long-term therapy effects.
Megaloblastic anemia due to folate or vitamin B12 deficiency may also be present.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS (part 2) | B

items.title: Neurologic Symptoms in Myeloma: Causes and Management
items.content: Neurologic symptoms in myeloma, though less common, can arise from hypercalcemia, hyperviscosity, bony damage, amyloid infiltration, or therapy side effects. Hypercalcemia may cause lethargy and confusion, while hyperviscosity can lead to headaches and visual disturbances. Therapy with thalidomide and bortezomib can cause sensory neuropathy.
keyPoints: Hypercalcemia in myeloma can produce lethargy, weakness, depression, and confusion.
Hyperviscosity may lead to headache, fatigue, shortness of breath, visual disturbances, ataxia, vertigo, retinopathy, somnolence, and coma.
Therapy with thalidomide and bortezomib can cause sensory neuropathy.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS (part 2) | B

items.title: Criteria for Myeloma Diagnosis and Progression Risk Factors in MGUS
items.content: The diagnosis of myeloma requires marrow plasmacytosis (>10%), a serum and/or urine M component, and at least one myeloma-defining event. Differentiating myeloma from MGUS or SMM is crucial due to varying progression risks. MGUS occurs in 1% of individuals over 50 years old and up to 10% over 75 years old. Progression risk factors for MGUS include non-IgG subtype, abnormal kappa/lambda free light chain ratio, and serum M protein >15 g/L (1.5 g/dL). Absence of these features predicts a 5% progression chance, while their presence predicts a 60% chance over 20 years.
keyPoints: Myeloma diagnosis requires marrow plasmacytosis (>10%), serum/urine M component, and one myeloma-defining event.
MGUS occurs in 1% of individuals >50 years old and up to 10% >75 years old.
Progression risk factors for MGUS: non-IgG subtype, abnormal kappa/lambda ratio, serum M protein >15 g/L (1.5 g/dL).
Absence of progression features predicts a 5% chance; presence predicts a 60% chance over 20 years.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · DIAGNOSIS AND STAGING

items.title: Risk Factors and Characteristics of Solitary Plasmacytomas
items.content: Solitary bone plasmacytoma and solitary extramedullary plasmacytoma are variants of myeloma with distinct features. They are associated with an M component in <30% of cases, may affect younger individuals, and have a median survival ≥10 years. Solitary bone plasmacytoma presents as a single lytic bone lesion without marrow plasmacytosis, while extramedullary plasmacytomas involve submucosal lymphoid tissue without marrow involvement. Both are highly responsive to local radiation therapy, with the M component disappearing post-treatment if present. Solitary bone plasmacytomas may recur or evolve into myeloma, whereas extramedullary plasmacytomas rarely progress.
keyPoints: Solitary plasmacytomas: <30% associated with M component, median survival ≥10 years.
Solitary bone plasmacytoma: single lytic bone lesion without marrow plasmacytosis.
Extramedullary plasmacytoma: involves submucosal lymphoid tissue without marrow involvement.
Both are responsive to local radiation therapy; M component should disappear post-treatment if present.
Solitary bone plasmacytomas may recur or evolve into myeloma; extramedullary rarely progress.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · DIAGNOSIS AND STAGING

items.title: Detecting M Spikes in Plasma Cell Disorders
items.content: Serum protein electrophoresis, immunoglobulin measurements, and free light chain assays are essential for detecting M spikes. Immunoelectrophoresis is particularly sensitive for low concentrations of M components undetectable by standard protein electrophoresis.
keyPoints: Serum protein electrophoresis and measurement of serum immunoglobulins and free light chains detect M spikes.
Immunoelectrophoresis identifies low concentrations of M components not detectable by protein electrophoresis.
A 24-h urine specimen is necessary to quantitate Bence Jones protein excretion.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATION

items.title: Characteristics and Detection Challenges of Light Chains in Myeloma
items.content: Light chains are often undetectable by standard tests, requiring specific assays. Serum free light chain assay is crucial for detecting light chains when urine tests fail.
keyPoints: 20% of patients have only light chains in serum and urine.
Dipsticks for proteinuria are unreliable for light chains; heat test for Bence Jones protein is falsely negative ~50% of the time.
Serum free light chain assay detects light chains when urine tests fail.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATION

items.title: Role of Serum β₂-Microglobulin in Myeloma Staging and Prognosis
items.content: Serum β₂-microglobulin is a powerful predictor of survival, forming the basis for the International Staging System (ISS) when combined with albumin levels.
keyPoints: Serum β₂-microglobulin is the most powerful predictor of survival in myeloma.
Combination of serum β₂-microglobulin and albumin levels forms the ISS for predicting survival.
Durie-Salmon staging system is no longer used due to inability to predict outcomes with newer therapies.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATION

items.subtopic: PROGNOSIS | MGUS, SMM, AND SOLITARY PLASMACYTOMA
items.title: Prognostic Factors in Plasma Cell Disorders
items.content: In plasma cell disorders like multiple myeloma, certain genetic abnormalities and biomarkers are associated with high-risk disease. These include chromosome 17p deletion with ≥20% clonality or TP53 mutation, translocations t(4;14), (14;16), or t(14;20) in conjunction with del 1p or amp1q changes, and elevated β2-microglobulin levels despite normal renal function. Conversely, chromosome 13q deletion and t(11;14) are not considered high-risk predictors.
keyPoints: Chromosome 17p deletion with ≥20% clonality or TP53 mutation is a high-risk factor.
Translocations t(4;14), (14;16), or t(14;20) associated with del 1p or amp1q changes are high-risk indicators.
High β2-microglobulin levels with normal renal function indicate high risk.
Chromosome 13q deletion and t(11;14) do not predict high-risk myeloma.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · PROGNOSIS | MGUS, SMM, AND SOLITARY PLASMACYTOMA

items.subtopic: PROGNOSIS | MGUS, SMM, AND SOLITARY PLASMACYTOMA
items.title: Management of MGUS and SMM
items.content: For patients with Monoclonal Gammopathy of Undetermined Significance (MGUS), no specific intervention is generally required. Annual follow-up suffices unless higher-risk features are present, necessitating more frequent monitoring. In Symptomatic Smoldering Myeloma (SMM), early intervention with lenalidomide alone or combined with dexamethasone can prevent progression to active multiple myeloma in high-risk cases.
keyPoints: No specific intervention for MGUS; annual follow-up is adequate unless higher risk.
Higher-risk MGUS requires monitoring every 6 months, including serum protein electrophoresis and renal function tests.
10% of patients with SMM have an indolent course but may benefit from early intervention to prevent progression.
Lenalidomide alone or with dexamethasone can prevent high-risk SMM progression to active MM.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · PROGNOSIS | MGUS, SMM, AND SOLITARY PLASMACYTOMA

items.title: Preferred Induction Regimens for Transplant-Eligible Myeloma Patients
items.content: In transplant-eligible myeloma patients, the combination of lenalidomide with a proteasome inhibitor (bortezomib or carfilzomib) and dexamethasone is preferred due to its high efficacy. This regimen achieves close to a 100% response rate and over 50% complete response rate.
keyPoints: Combination of lenalidomide, proteasome inhibitor (bortezomib or carfilzomib), and dexamethasone is preferred for transplant-eligible patients.
Achieves close to a 100% response rate.
Over 50% complete response rate.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC MM

items.title: Induction Therapy in Non-Transplant Candidates
items.content: For myeloma patients who are not transplant candidates due to age or comorbidities, three-drug combinations such as bortezomib with thalidomide and dexamethasone or bortezomib with cyclophosphamide and dexamethasone remain standard. These regimens achieve over 90% response rates.
keyPoints: Standard induction therapy for non-transplant candidates includes three-drug combinations like bortezomib, thalidomide, and dexamethasone or bortezomib, cyclophosphamide, and dexamethasone.
Achieves over 90% response rate.
Dose and schedule modifications are guided by age and frailty.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC MM

items.subtopic: HIGH-DOSE THERAPY WITH AUTOLOGOUS STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION
items.title: Timing and Efficacy of High-Dose Therapy in Myeloma Patients
items.content: High-dose therapy (HDT) with hematopoietic stem cell support is standard for eligible myeloma patients, offering higher response rates and prolonged survival. Stem cells should be collected within 6 months if lenalidomide is used pre-transplant due to its impact on stem cell collection.
keyPoints: HDT achieves up to 25–40% additional complete responses (CRs) over standard-dose therapy.
Prolonged progression-free and overall survival with HDT.
Stem cells should be collected within 6 months if lenalidomide is used pre-transplant.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · HIGH-DOSE THERAPY WITH AUTOLOGOUS STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION

items.subtopic: HIGH-DOSE THERAPY WITH AUTOLOGOUS STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION
items.title: Maintenance Therapy Post-HDT in Myeloma Management
items.content: Post-HDT maintenance therapy, particularly with lenalidomide, significantly improves progression-free survival and overall survival. It is effective even for nontransplant candidates, though it carries a small increased risk of second primary malignancies.
keyPoints: Lenalidomide maintenance post-HDT shows improved progression-free and overall survival.
Nontransplant candidates benefit from lenalidomide maintenance after induction therapy.
Increased incidence of second primary malignancies is outweighed by benefits in reducing myeloma progression.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · HIGH-DOSE THERAPY WITH AUTOLOGOUS STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION

items.title: Treatment Options for Relapsed Myeloma
items.content: Relapsed myeloma can be treated with a variety of agents, including lenalidomide and bortezomib if not previously used. Second-generation proteasome inhibitors like carfilzomib and immunomodulatory agents such as pomalidomide are effective even in cases refractory to lenalidomide and bortezomib. Ixazomib, an oral proteasome inhibitor, is approved for use with lenalidomide and dexamethasone.
keyPoints: Lenalidomide and/or bortezomib can be used if not previously administered.
Carfilzomib and pomalidomide are effective in relapsed/refractory MM, even when refractory to lenalidomide and bortezomib.
Ixazomib is approved for an all-oral regimen with lenalidomide and dexamethasone.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · RELAPSED DISEASE

items.title: Monoclonal Antibodies in Relapsed Myeloma
items.content: Daratumumab, targeting CD38, achieves high response rates and improved progression-free survival. It is more effective when combined with bortezomib/dexamethasone or lenalidomide/dexamethasone. Isatuximab also targets CD38 and shows efficacy in combination therapies. Elotuzumab targets SLAMF7 and is active in combination but not as a single agent. Belantamab mafodotin, targeting BCMA, delivers auristatin to tumor cells and requires monitoring for ophthalmologic toxicity.
keyPoints: Daratumumab improves response rates and progression-free survival; more effective when combined with bortezomib/dexamethasone or lenalidomide/dexamethasone.
Isatuximab targets CD38, showing high response rates in combination therapies.
Elotuzumab targets SLAMF7, active in combination but not as a single agent.
Belantamab mafodotin targets BCMA and has unique ophthalmologic toxicity.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · RELAPSED DISEASE

items.title: Immunotherapeutic Approaches for Relapsed Myeloma
items.content: CAR T-cell therapies targeting BCMA, such as idecabtagene vicleucel (Ide-cel) and ciltacabtagene autoleucel (Cilta-cel), are approved after specific prior treatments. Ide-cel shows an 81% response rate with a CR rate of 33%, while Cilta-cel achieves 98% overall response with 82% achieving CR. Bispecific antibodies like teclistamab, elranatamab, and talquetamab target BCMA or GPRC5D, resulting in 60–70% response rates with a quarter of patients achieving CR.
keyPoints: Ide-cel (after at least two prior lines including an immunomodulatory agent, proteasome inhibitor, and anti-CD38) shows 81% response rate, 33% CR.
Cilta-cel (after one prior line including a proteasome inhibitor and immunomodulatory agent) achieves 98% overall response, 82% CR.
Bispecific antibodies target BCMA or GPRC5D with 60–70% response rates; quarter achieve CR.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · RELAPSED DISEASE

items.title: Understanding Therapy Endpoints in Plasma Cell Disorders
items.content: In plasma cell disorders, achieving complete remission (CR) is a traditional therapy endpoint. CR is defined by the disappearance of serum and urine monoclonal protein with normal bone marrow as assessed by light microscopy. However, minimal residual disease (MRD) assessment using multicolor flow cytometry offers a more sensitive measure, detecting one myeloma cell in a million. Absence of MRD predicts longer progression-free survival and overall survival.
keyPoints: Complete remission (CR) is defined as the disappearance of serum and urine monoclonal protein with normal bone marrow by light microscopy.
Minimal residual disease (MRD) assessment detects one myeloma cell in a million, predicting longer progression-free and overall survival.
Absence of MRD at high sensitivity is an important new endpoint for both newly diagnosed and relapsed patients.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · THERAPY ENDPOINT

items.title: Survival Outcomes and Causes of Death in Myeloma Patients
items.content: The median overall survival for myeloma patients is over 8 years, with younger subsets potentially surviving more than 10 years. Major causes of death include progressive myeloma, renal failure, sepsis, or therapy-related myelodysplasia. A significant proportion (nearly a quarter) die from intercurrent illnesses such as myocardial infarction, chronic lung disease, diabetes, or stroke, which are more related to patient age than the tumor itself.
keyPoints: Median overall survival for myeloma patients is over 8 years; younger patients may survive >10 years.
Major causes of death: progressive myeloma, renal failure, sepsis, therapy-related myelodysplasia.
Nearly a quarter of deaths are due to intercurrent illnesses (myocardial infarction, chronic lung disease, diabetes, stroke) related more to age than the tumor.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · THERAPY ENDPOINT

items.title: Herpes Zoster Prophylaxis with Bortezomib
items.content: Bortezomib use necessitates prophylaxis against herpes zoster due to increased risk of reactivation.
keyPoints: Herpes zoster prophylaxis is indicated if bortezomib is used.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · SUPPORTIVE THERAPY

items.title: Management of Hypercalcemia in Myeloma
items.content: Hypercalcemia in myeloma patients can be effectively managed with bisphosphonates, glucocorticoid therapy, hydration, and natriuresis.
keyPoints: Bisphosphonates (e.g., pamidronate 90 mg or zoledronate 4 mg initially once a month for 12–24 months) reduce osteoclastic bone resorption.
Denosumab is an alternative to bisphosphonates, administered at 120 mg monthly.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · SUPPORTIVE THERAPY

items.title: Prophylaxis for Deep-Vein Thrombosis with Lenalidomide
items.content: Lenalidomide therapy requires prophylactic measures against DVT, using aspirin or stronger anticoagulants in higher-risk patients.
keyPoints: Lenalidomide use requires prophylaxis for deep-vein thrombosis (DVT) with either aspirin or, if at greater risk, warfarin, low-molecular-weight heparin, or DOACs.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · SUPPORTIVE THERAPY

items.title: Diagnostic Criteria and Molecular Pathogenesis
items.content: Waldenström's macroglobulinemia (WM) is characterized by a distinct MYD88 L265P mutation present in >90% of patients, which aids in differentiating it from other conditions like marginal zone lymphomas. This mutation leads to activation of signaling pathways involving IRAK and NF-$\kappa$B, crucial for the disease's pathogenesis.
keyPoints: MYD88 L265P mutation present in >90% of patients
Differentiates WM from MZLs, IgM-secreting myeloma, and CLL with plasmacytoid differentiation
Activates IRAK4/1 and NF-$\kappa$B signaling pathways
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · WALDENSTRÖM'S MACROGLOBULINEMIA

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Laboratory Findings
items.content: Patients with WM often present with symptoms of hyperviscosity syndrome, including visual disturbances and neurologic symptoms. The disease involves bone marrow infiltration by lymphoplasmacytic cells without causing bone lesions or hypercalcemia. Serum M component is typically >30 g/L (3 g/dL), but renal excretion is minimal.
keyPoints: Symptoms include weakness, fatigue, recurrent infections, epistaxis, visual disturbances, and neuropathy
Bone marrow shows >10% infiltration with lymphoplasmacytic cells
Serum M component usually >30 g/L (3 g/dL), minimal renal excretion
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · WALDENSTRÖM'S MACROGLOBULINEMIA

items.title: Genetic Mutations and Their Clinical Implications
items.content: In addition to the MYD88 mutation, CXCR4 mutations (30–40%) are associated with higher bone marrow disease burden and increased incidence of hyperviscosity. These genetic alterations influence clinical presentation and therapeutic targets.
keyPoints: CXCR4 mutations occur in 30-40% of patients
Associated with higher bone marrow disease burden and hyperviscosity
MYD88 and CXCR4 mutations guide treatment strategies
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · WALDENSTRÖM'S MACROGLOBULINEMIA

items.title: When and How to Treat Waldenström’s Macroglobulinemia (WM)
items.content: Treatment for WM is generally initiated only if the patient presents with symptoms or complications such as anemia, hyperviscosity, lymphadenopathy, or hepatosplenomegaly. Acute management of serious hyperviscosity can be achieved through plasmapheresis due to its ability to rapidly reduce intravascular IgM levels.
keyPoints: Treatment is initiated if symptomatic or with complications like anemia and hyperviscosity.
Plasmapheresis acutely controls severe hyperviscosity symptoms, reducing 80% of the intravascular IgM paraprotein.
Median survival for WM patients is ~50 months.
High-risk population defined by older age, male sex, general symptoms, and cytopenias.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · Waldenström's Macroglobulinemia

items.title: First-Line Therapy Considerations in Symptomatic WM Patients
items.content: For symptomatic WM patients, first-line therapy often includes BTK inhibitors like ibrutinib, alkylating drugs (bendamustine and cyclophosphamide), proteasome inhibitors (bortezomib, carfilzomib, ixazomib) in combination with rituximab. Ibrutinib shows a high response rate of 91% after one line of therapy, particularly effective in patients with mutated MYD88 and wild-type CXCR4.
keyPoints: First-line therapies include BTK inhibitors (ibrutinib), alkylating drugs, proteasome inhibitors combined with rituximab.
Ibrutinib has an overall response rate of 91% after one prior line of therapy.
Best responses to ibrutinib are seen in patients with mutated MYD88 and wild-type CXCR4.
Rituximab can cause IgM flare; plasmapheresis is recommended before rituximab or withhold its use initially if IgM levels are high.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · Waldenström's Macroglobulinemia

items.title: Diagnostic Criteria and Clinical Features of POEMS Syndrome
items.content: POEMS syndrome is characterized by polyneuropathy, organomegaly, endocrinopathy, M-protein, and skin changes. It occurs in approximately 1.4% of myeloma cases but represents a rare subset. Key features include severe sensorimotor polyneuropathy, sclerotic bone lesions, hepatomegaly, lymphadenopathy (often resembling Castleman's disease), and various endocrine manifestations such as hyperprolactinemia and type 2 diabetes mellitus in about one-third of patients.
keyPoints: POEMS syndrome occurs in ~1.4% of myelomas
Hepatomegaly and lymphadenopathy occur in about two-thirds of patients
Splenomegaly is seen in one-third
Endocrine manifestations include amenorrhea, impotence, gynecomastia, hyperprolactinemia, type 2 diabetes mellitus (~1/3), hypothyroidism, and adrenal insufficiency occasionally
Skin changes: hyperpigmentation, hypertrichosis, skin thickening, digital clubbing
Peripheral edema, ascites, pleural effusions, fever, thrombocytosis may also be present
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · POEMS SYNDROME | HEAVY CHAIN DISEASES

items.title: Treatment Approaches for POEMS Syndrome
items.content: The management of POEMS syndrome often parallels that of myeloma, focusing on treating the underlying plasma cell disorder. Plasmapheresis is not beneficial. Local therapy with radiotherapy can resolve neuropathic symptoms in patients with isolated sclerotic lesions. Advanced treatments include novel agents and high-dose therapy (HDT) with autologous stem cell transplantation, which have been linked to prolonged progression-free survival.
keyPoints: Treatment parallels myeloma management
Plasmapheresis is not beneficial
Local radiotherapy may resolve neuropathic symptoms in isolated sclerotic lesions
Novel agents and HDT with autologous stem cell transplantation can lead to prolonged progression-free survival
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · POEMS SYNDROME | HEAVY CHAIN DISEASES

items.title: Recognize and Diagnose Gamma Heavy Chain Disease
items.content: Gamma heavy chain disease is characterized by symptoms such as lymphadenopathy, fever, anemia, malaise, hepatosplenomegaly, weakness, and distinctive palatal edema. Diagnosis involves identifying a serum M component that reacts with anti-IgG but not anti-light chain reagents.
keyPoints: Symptoms: lymphadenopathy, fever, anemia, malaise, hepatosplenomegaly, weakness, palatal edema
Autoimmune association: especially rheumatoid arthritis
Serum M component: <20 g/L [<2 g/dL], reacts with anti-IgG but not anti-light chain reagents
Paraproteins: typically $\gamma_1$ subclass
Therapy: indicated when symptomatic, involves chemotherapeutic combinations used in low-grade lymphoma; Rituximab shows efficacy
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · GAMMA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (FRANKLIN'S DISEASE) | ALPHA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (SELIGMANN'S DISEASE) | MU HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE

items.title: Understand the Clinical Presentation and Management of Alpha Heavy Chain Disease
items.content: Alpha heavy chain disease, often linked to Mediterranean lymphoma, presents with chronic diarrhea, weight loss, malabsorption, and mesenteric/paraaortic adenopathy. Diagnosis is challenging due to alpha chains polymerizing on electrophoretic profiles.
keyPoints: Common in regions with intestinal parasites: Mediterranean, Asia, South America
Symptoms: chronic diarrhea, weight loss, malabsorption, extensive mesenteric and paraaortic adenopathy
Alpha chain characteristics: difficult to demonstrate due to polymerization; no hyperviscosity issues
Light chains: absent from serum and urine
Treatment: chemotherapy may produce long-term remissions; antibiotics effective in IPSID (30–70% complete remission)
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · GAMMA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (FRANKLIN'S DISEASE) | ALPHA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (SELIGMANN'S DISEASE) | MU HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE

items.title: Identify Mu Heavy Chain Disease in CLL Patients
items.content: Mu heavy chain disease is rare and occurs in a subset of patients with chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL). It involves the secretion of isolated mu heavy chains into serum, distinguishable by vacuoles in malignant lymphocytes and kappa light chain excretion.
keyPoints: Occurrence: rare subset of CLL
Diagnostic features: presence of vacuoles in malignant lymphocytes, excretion of kappa light chains in urine
Diagnosis: requires ultracentrifugation or gel filtration to confirm nonreactivity with light chain reagents
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · GAMMA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (FRANKLIN'S DISEASE) | ALPHA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (SELIGMANN'S DISEASE) | MU HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a synonym for plasma cell disorders?
items.explanation: The passage lists monoclonal gammopathies, paraproteinemias, plasma cell dyscrasias, and dysproteinemias as synonyms for plasma cell disorders.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · Plasma Cell Disorders (part 1)

items.question: What is the role of antigen exposure in normal B lymphocyte maturation?
options: It stimulates antibody-secreting plasma cells and their proliferation
It has no effect on maturation
items.explanation: Under normal circumstances, maturation to antibody-secreting plasma cells and their proliferation is stimulated by exposure to the antigen.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · Plasma Cell Disorders (part 1)

items.question: What distinguishes idiotypic determinants from other immunoglobulin structural variations?
options: They are recognized by antibodies from a distinct species
They reflect small differences between individuals of the same species
They are unique to molecules produced by a given clone
They distinguish among main classes of antibodies
items.explanation: Idiotypes are unique to the molecules produced by a given clone and form the antigen-binding portion.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · Plasma Cell Disorders (part 1)

items.question: Which heavy chain isotype is NOT mentioned in the passage?
items.explanation: The passage mentions five heavy chain isotypes: M, G, A, D, E.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · Plasma Cell Disorders (part 1)

items.question: Which method provides a qualitative assessment of the M component in plasma cell disorders?
items.explanation: Immunoelectrophoresis reveals a single heavy and/or light chain type, providing qualitative assessment.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · Plasma Cell Disorders (part 2)

items.question: In which region of the serum proteins does an M component typically appear?
items.explanation: The M component forms a sharp spike in the gamma region.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · Plasma Cell Disorders (part 2)

items.question: What is the minimum concentration of monoclonal antibody required for accurate quantitation by electrophoretic analysis?
items.explanation: The monoclonal antibody must be present at a concentration of at least 5 g/L (0.5 g/dL).
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · Plasma Cell Disorders (part 2)

items.question: Which chromosomal translocation involving chromosome 14 is commonly observed in myeloma patients?
items.explanation: Translocation $ t(11;14)(q13;q32) $ involving chromosome 14 is commonly observed in myeloma patients.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following genes is most frequently mutated in myeloma?
items.explanation: The KRAS and NRAS genes are each mutated in approximately 20% of myeloma cases, making them the most frequently mutated.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY

items.question: What is a potential environmental risk factor for developing myeloma?
items.explanation: Myeloma occurred with increased frequency in those exposed to the radiation of nuclear warheads.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which population group has the highest incidence of myeloma according to global considerations?
options: People from developing countries including Asia
items.explanation: The incidence of myeloma is highest in blacks and Pacific Islanders.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.question: What factor contributes to the higher incidence of myeloma in more developed countries?
options: More frequent medical surveillance and longer life expectancy
items.explanation: The higher incidence in more developed countries may result from the combination of a longer life expectancy and more frequent medical surveillance.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which ethnic groups have a higher incidence of myeloma relative to U.S. whites in the same geographic area?
options: Native Hawaiians, female Hispanics, American Indians from New Mexico, and Alaskan natives
People from developing countries including Asia
items.explanation: Incidence of MM in other ethnic groups including native Hawaiians, female Hispanics, American Indians from New Mexico, and Alaskan natives is higher relative to U.S. whites.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which populations have a lower incidence of myeloma compared to whites?
options: Native Hawaiians, female Hispanics, American Indians from New Mexico, and Alaskan natives
People from developing countries including Asia
items.explanation: Chinese and Japanese populations have a lower incidence than whites.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.question: What is the most common cause of renal failure in myeloma patients?
items.explanation: Hypercalcemia is noted as the most common cause of renal failure in myeloma patients.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS (part 2) | B

items.question: Which condition is an early manifestation of tubular damage due to light chain excretion in myeloma?
items.explanation: The earliest manifestation of tubular damage from light chains is adult Fanconi's syndrome.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS (part 2) | B

items.question: What type of anemia is most commonly observed in myeloma patients?
items.explanation: Normocytic and normochromic anemia occurs in ~80% of myeloma patients.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS (part 2) | B

items.question: Which paraprotein type is most commonly associated with hyperviscosity syndromes?
items.explanation: Hyperviscosity syndromes are most common with IgM, IgG3, and IgA paraproteins.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS (part 2) | B

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a criterion for diagnosing multiple myeloma?
options: Presence of serum and/or urine M component
Absence of bone marrow plasma cells
items.explanation: Diagnosis requires marrow plasmacytosis, an M component, and a myeloma-defining event.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · DIAGNOSIS AND STAGING

items.question: Which feature is associated with a higher risk of progression from MGUS to myeloma?
options: Normal kappa/lambda free light chain ratio
Absence of all three high-risk features
items.explanation: Non-IgG subtype, abnormal kappa/lambda ratio, and serum M protein >15 g/L are associated with higher progression risk.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · DIAGNOSIS AND STAGING

items.question: What is the chance of progression to myeloma for patients with high-risk SMM over 5 years?
items.explanation: High-risk SMM (all three features) has a 76% chance of progression in 5 years.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · DIAGNOSIS AND STAGING

items.question: Which immunoglobulin is most commonly found as the serum M component in patients?
items.explanation: IgG is the serum M component in 53% of patients.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATION

items.question: Which test is not reliable for detecting light chains in urine?
items.explanation: Dipsticks are not reliable at identifying light chains.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATION

items.question: What percentage of patients with IgM paraproteins develop hyperviscosity?
items.explanation: About half of patients with IgM paraproteins develop hyperviscosity.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATION

items.question: Which imaging technique is considered standard for distinguishing between smoldering and active multiple myeloma?
items.explanation: $^{18}$F-FDG PET/CT is considered standard for distinguishing between smoldering and active MM.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATION

items.subtopic: PROGNOSIS | MGUS, SMM, AND SOLITARY PLASMACYTOMA
items.question: Which of the following is NOT considered a high-risk prognostic factor for myeloma?
options: Chromosome 17p deletion with 20% clonality or TP53 mutation
Translocations t(4;14), (14;16), or t(14;20) associated with del 1p or amp1q changes
items.explanation: Chromosome 13q deletion is not considered a predictor of high-risk myeloma.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · PROGNOSIS | MGUS, SMM, AND SOLITARY PLASMACYTOMA

items.subtopic: PROGNOSIS | MGUS, SMM, AND SOLITARY PLASMACYTOMA
items.question: What is the recommended follow-up frequency for patients with higher-risk MGUS?
options: Once a year or less frequently
items.explanation: Higher-risk MGUS requires follow-up every 6 months.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · PROGNOSIS | MGUS, SMM, AND SOLITARY PLASMACYTOMA

items.subtopic: PROGNOSIS | MGUS, SMM, AND SOLITARY PLASMACYTOMA
items.question: Which therapy is considered for patients with IgM MGUS or myeloma-like disease?
options: Lenalidomide alone or in combination with dexamethasone
Local radiation therapy at a dose of $ \sim $40 Gy
items.explanation: Plasmapheresis and occasionally rituximab are considered for IgM MGUS or myeloma-like disease.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · PROGNOSIS | MGUS, SMM, AND SOLITARY PLASMACYTOMA

items.question: Which combination is preferred as an induction regimen in transplant-eligible patients for newly diagnosed myeloma?
options: Lenalidomide, bortezomib or carfilzomib, and dexamethasone
items.explanation: The combination of lenalidomide with a proteasome inhibitor (bortezomib or carfilzomib) and dexamethasone is preferred for transplant-eligible patients.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC MM

items.question: What is the standard induction therapy for myeloma patients who are not transplant candidates due to age or comorbidities?
options: High-dose therapy with autologous stem cell transplantation
items.explanation: The combination of daratumumab with lenalidomide and dexamethasone (DRd) is standard for non-transplant candidates, with dose modifications.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC MM

items.question: Which therapy combinations are no longer utilized as effectively due to the availability of newer agents?
options: Melphalan with prednisone alone or with thalidomide or bortezomib
items.explanation: MP-based combinations are no longer utilized effectively due to newer agents.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC MM

items.subtopic: HIGH-DOSE THERAPY WITH AUTOLOGOUS STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION
items.question: When should stem cells be collected in patients receiving lenalidomide prior to high-dose therapy with autologous stem cell transplantation?
options: At any time during the treatment course
Only if the patient does not respond to lenalidomide
items.explanation: Stem cells should be collected within 6 months because continued use of lenalidomide may compromise stem cell collection.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · HIGH-DOSE THERAPY WITH AUTOLOGOUS STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION

items.subtopic: HIGH-DOSE THERAPY WITH AUTOLOGOUS STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION
items.question: What is the observed benefit of tandem transplantation compared to single high-dose therapy?
options: It only benefits those who do not achieve a complete or very good partial response after the first transplantation.
It significantly increases overall survival for all patients.
There is no benefit observed in any subset of patients.
items.explanation: Tandem transplantation is more effective only in patients who do not achieve a complete or very good partial response to the first transplantation.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · HIGH-DOSE THERAPY WITH AUTOLOGOUS STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION

items.subtopic: HIGH-DOSE THERAPY WITH AUTOLOGOUS STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION
items.question: What has been demonstrated by phase 3 studies regarding lenalidomide maintenance therapy after high-dose therapy?
options: It reduces the risk of second primary malignancies.
It shows no significant improvement in progression-free survival.
It prolongs overall survival compared to placebo.
It is only beneficial for nontransplant candidates.
items.explanation: One phase 3 study showed prolonged overall survival with lenalidomide maintenance therapy after high-dose therapy.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · HIGH-DOSE THERAPY WITH AUTOLOGOUS STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION

items.question: Which antibody-drug conjugate targets B-cell maturation antigen (BCMA) and delivers auristatin to tumor cells in relapsed/refractory myeloma?
items.explanation: Belantamab mafodotin targets BCMA and delivers auristatin to tumor cells.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · RELAPSED DISEASE

items.question: Which of the following agents is a first-in-class exportin inhibitor approved for relapsed/refractory myeloma?
items.explanation: Selinexor is a first-in-class exportin inhibitor approved for relapsed/refractory myeloma.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · RELAPSED DISEASE

items.question: Which cellular therapy targeting BCMA requires at least two prior lines of therapy including an immunomodulatory agent, proteasome inhibitor, and anti-CD38 monoclonal antibody?
items.explanation: Idecabtagene vicleucel requires at least two prior lines including an immunomodulatory agent, a proteasome inhibitor, and an anti-CD38 monoclonal antibody.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · RELAPSED DISEASE

items.question: Which bispecific antibody targets G protein-coupled receptor class C group 5 (GPRC5D) in relapsed refractory myeloma?
items.explanation: Talquetamab targets GPRC5D on myeloma cells.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · RELAPSED DISEASE

items.question: Which factor contributes to the lag in improvement of serum M component compared to symptomatic improvement?
options: Shorter serum half-life of immunoglobulin (~3 weeks)
Longer serum half-life of immunoglobulin (~3 weeks)
Immediate fall of serum and urine light chains
items.explanation: The longer serum half-life (~3 weeks) of the immunoglobulin contributes to the lag in improvement.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · THERAPY ENDPOINT

items.question: What is a standard goal of therapy for myeloma?
options: Achieving partial remission with detectable monoclonal protein
Disappearance of serum and urine monoclonal protein with normal bone marrow by light microscopy
Presence of minimal residual disease (MRD) in bone marrow
items.explanation: Achieving complete remission, defined as disappearance of serum and urine monoclonal protein with normal bone marrow by light microscopy, is a standard goal.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · THERAPY ENDPOINT

items.question: Why are urine light chain levels not reliable in patients with renal dysfunction?
options: They have a longer functional half-life (~3 weeks)
They do not relate to renal tubular function
They may fall much quicker within the first week of treatment
Their measurement is affected by renal tubular function
items.explanation: Urine light chain levels may fall quickly and are related to renal tubular function, making them unreliable in patients with renal dysfunction.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · THERAPY ENDPOINT

items.question: Which medication is recommended for prophylaxis in patients receiving lenalidomide who are at a higher risk of deep-vein thrombosis (DVT)?
items.explanation: Patients at a greater risk of DVT should receive warfarin, low-molecular-weight heparin, or DOACs.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · SUPPORTIVE THERAPY

items.question: What is the initial recommended dose and frequency for pamidronate in bisphosphonate therapy?
items.explanation: The initial regimen is 90 mg of pamidronate once a month for 12–24 months.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · SUPPORTIVE THERAPY

items.question: Which therapy may require dose modification in patients with renal failure?
items.explanation: Lenalidomide requires dose modification as it is renally excreted.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · SUPPORTIVE THERAPY

items.question: What treatment is more effective at clearing light chains in acute renal failure?
items.explanation: Plasmapheresis is approximately 10 times more effective at clearing light chains than peritoneal dialysis.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · SUPPORTIVE THERAPY

items.question: Which mutation is present in over 90% of patients with Waldenström's macroglobulinemia and is used as a diagnostic test to differentiate it from other lymphomas?
items.explanation: The MYD88 L265P mutation is present in over 90% of patients with Waldenström's macroglobulinemia and helps differentiate it from other lymphomas.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · WALDENSTRÖM'S MACROGLOBULINEMIA

items.question: Which clinical manifestation is more common in Waldenström's macroglobulinemia compared to myeloma?
items.explanation: Epistaxis and visual disturbances are more common in Waldenström's macroglobulinemia compared to myeloma.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · WALDENSTRÖM'S MACROGLOBULINEMIA

items.question: What is a characteristic feature of the bone marrow in patients with Waldenström's macroglobulinemia?
items.explanation: Bone marrow in Waldenström's macroglobulinemia shows >10% infiltration with lymphoplasmacytic cells.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · WALDENSTRÖM'S MACROGLOBULINEMIA

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a common mutation associated with Waldenström's macroglobulinemia?
items.explanation: BRAF V600E is not mentioned as a common mutation associated with Waldenström's macroglobulinemia.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · WALDENSTRÖM'S MACROGLOBULINEMIA

items.question: Which of the following is a primary target for ibrutinib in the treatment of Waldenström's macroglobulinemia?
items.explanation: Ibrutinib targets the constitutively activated BTK.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · Waldenström's Macroglobulinemia

items.question: Which of these treatments is NOT recommended as a first-line therapy for symptomatic patients with Waldenström's macroglobulinemia?
items.explanation: HDT plus autologous transplantation is not recommended due to the availability of other effective agents.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · Waldenström's Macroglobulinemia

items.question: What percentage of IgM paraprotein in Waldenström's macroglobulinemia is intravascular, allowing acute control of hyperviscosity symptoms by plasmapheresis?
items.explanation: 80% of the IgM paraprotein is intravascular.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · Waldenström's Macroglobulinemia

items.question: Which cytokine is overproduced in POEMS syndrome and has been linked to histological features resembling Castleman's disease?
items.explanation: IL-6 overproduction is linked to lymphadenopathy resembling Castleman's disease in POEMS syndrome.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · POEMS SYNDROME | HEAVY CHAIN DISEASES

items.question: Which of the following skin changes is NOT typically associated with POEMS syndrome?
items.explanation: Psoriasis is not listed as a typical skin change in POEMS syndrome.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · POEMS SYNDROME | HEAVY CHAIN DISEASES

items.question: Which treatment has been associated with prolonged progression-free survival in selected patients with POEMS syndrome?
options: HDT with autologous stem cell transplantation
items.explanation: High-dose therapy (HDT) with autologous stem cell transplantation has been associated with prolonged progression-free survival in selected patients.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · POEMS SYNDROME | HEAVY CHAIN DISEASES

items.subtopic: GAMMA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (FRANKLIN'S DISEASE) | ALPHA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (SELIGMANN'S DISEASE) | MU HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE
items.question: Which heavy chain disease is most commonly associated with Mediterranean lymphoma?
items.explanation: Alpha Heavy Chain Disease is closely related to Mediterranean lymphoma.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · GAMMA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (FRANKLIN'S DISEASE) | ALPHA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (SELIGMANN'S DISEASE) | MU HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE

items.subtopic: GAMMA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (FRANKLIN'S DISEASE) | ALPHA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (SELIGMANN'S DISEASE) | MU HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE
items.question: What is a distinctive symptom of Gamma Heavy Chain Disease?
items.explanation: Gamma Heavy Chain Disease is characterized by palatal edema due to involvement of nodes in Waldeyer's ring.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · GAMMA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (FRANKLIN'S DISEASE) | ALPHA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (SELIGMANN'S DISEASE) | MU HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE

items.subtopic: GAMMA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (FRANKLIN'S DISEASE) | ALPHA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (SELIGMANN'S DISEASE) | MU HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE
items.question: Which heavy chain disease involves the secretion of truncated alpha chains?
items.explanation: Alpha Heavy Chain Disease is characterized by infiltration with lymphoplasmacytoid cells that secrete truncated alpha chains.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · GAMMA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (FRANKLIN'S DISEASE) | ALPHA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (SELIGMANN'S DISEASE) | MU HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE

items.subtopic: GAMMA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (FRANKLIN'S DISEASE) | ALPHA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (SELIGMANN'S DISEASE) | MU HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE
items.question: Which heavy chain disease is associated with the presence of vacuoles in malignant lymphocytes?
items.explanation: Mu Heavy Chain Disease may be distinguished by vacuoles in malignant lymphocytes.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · GAMMA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (FRANKLIN'S DISEASE) | ALPHA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (SELIGMANN'S DISEASE) | MU HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE

items.statement: Normal development of B lymphocytes involves exposure to antigen for maturation.
items.explanation: Under normal circumstances, maturation to antibody-secreting plasma cells and their proliferation is stimulated by exposure to the antigen.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · Plasma Cell Disorders (part 1)

items.statement: Isotypic determinants are recognized by antibodies from the same species.
items.explanation: Isotypic determinants are recognized by heterologous sera, not homologous sera.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · Plasma Cell Disorders (part 1)

items.statement: M components are specific enough to be used for screening asymptomatic patients.
items.explanation: The M component is not specific enough to be used to screen asymptomatic patients.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · Plasma Cell Disorders (part 2)

items.statement: In approximately 20% of myelomas, only light chains are produced.
items.explanation: ~20% of myelomas produce only light chains, secreted as Bence Jones proteins.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · Plasma Cell Disorders (part 2)

items.statement: A single common molecular pathogenic pathway has been identified for myeloma.
items.explanation: No single common molecular pathogenic pathway has yet emerged in the study of myeloma.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY

items.statement: Interleukin 6 may play a role in driving myeloma cell proliferation.
items.explanation: IL-6 is suggested to contribute to the proliferation of myeloma cells.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY

items.statement: The characteristics, response to therapy, and prognosis of myeloma are similar worldwide.
items.explanation: Despite differences in prevalence, the characteristics, response to therapy, and prognosis of myeloma are similar worldwide.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Myeloma is more common in individuals under age 40.
items.explanation: Myeloma is uncommon under age 40.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Renal dysfunction in myeloma patients is always irreversible.
items.explanation: Renal dysfunction due to light chain deposition disease, light chain cast nephropathy, and amyloidosis is partially reversible with effective therapy.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS (part 2) | B

items.statement: Thrombocytopenia in myeloma patients is common without therapy.
items.explanation: Granulocytopenia and thrombocytopenia are rare except when therapy-induced.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS (part 2) | B

items.statement: All cases of myeloma are preceded by MGUS.
items.explanation: Myeloma always follows a phase of MGUS.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · DIAGNOSIS AND STAGING

items.statement: Solitary extramedullary plasmacytomas are not associated with an M component in most cases.
items.explanation: These lesions have an M component in <30% of the cases.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · DIAGNOSIS AND STAGING

items.statement: Serum alkaline phosphatase levels are usually elevated in patients with extensive bone involvement.
items.explanation: Serum alkaline phosphatase is usually normal even with extensive bone involvement.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATION

items.statement: The Durie-Salmon staging system is still used to predict outcomes in multiple myeloma.
items.explanation: The Durie-Salmon staging system is unable to predict outcome and is no longer used.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATION

items.subtopic: PROGNOSIS | MGUS, SMM, AND SOLITARY PLASMACYTOMA
items.statement: Patients with solitary bone plasmacytomas are expected to have prolonged disease-free survival after local radiation therapy.
items.explanation: Local radiation therapy at a dose of ~40 Gy is associated with prolonged disease-free survival in patients with solitary bone plasmacytomas.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · PROGNOSIS | MGUS, SMM, AND SOLITARY PLASMACYTOMA

items.subtopic: PROGNOSIS | MGUS, SMM, AND SOLITARY PLASMACYTOMA
items.statement: No consensus exists on the management of renal dysfunction in MGUS.
items.explanation: There is no consensus on managing renal dysfunction in MGUS, but lowering monoclonal antibody levels with bortezomib has some advocates.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · PROGNOSIS | MGUS, SMM, AND SOLITARY PLASMACYTOMA

items.statement: The addition of daratumumab or isatuximab as a fourth agent in induction therapy provides deeper responses.
items.explanation: Four-drug regimens including daratumumab or isatuximab are becoming standard due to their effectiveness.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC MM

items.statement: High-dose therapy with autologous stem cell transplantation is the initial treatment for all myeloma patients.
items.explanation: Initial treatment involves induction regimens, followed by high-dose therapy and transplantation if applicable.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC MM

items.subtopic: HIGH-DOSE THERAPY WITH AUTOLOGOUS STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION
items.statement: Allogeneic transplantation is commonly used due to its effectiveness and low toxicity.
items.explanation: Allogeneic transplantation produces high response rates but with significant toxicities, making it rarely used.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · HIGH-DOSE THERAPY WITH AUTOLOGOUS STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION

items.subtopic: HIGH-DOSE THERAPY WITH AUTOLOGOUS STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION
items.statement: Maintenance therapy can prolong progression-free survival after standard-dose regimens.
items.explanation: Maintenance therapy, including lenalidomide, has been shown to improve progression-free survival.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · HIGH-DOSE THERAPY WITH AUTOLOGOUS STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION

items.statement: Panobinostat, when combined with bortezomib and dexamethasone, has shown superior response compared to the combination of bortezomib and dexamethasone alone in relapsed/refractory myeloma.
items.explanation: Panobinostat shows superior response when combined with bortezomib and dexamethasone.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · RELAPSED DISEASE

items.statement: Cytokine release syndrome is not a primary toxicity associated with bispecific antibodies.
items.explanation: Cytokine release syndrome is a primary toxicity of bispecific antibodies.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · RELAPSED DISEASE

items.statement: Achieving complete remission (CR) is no longer considered a standard goal of therapy for myeloma.
items.explanation: Achieving CR, defined as disappearance of serum and urine monoclonal protein with normal bone marrow by light microscopy, remains a standard goal.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · THERAPY ENDPOINT

items.statement: Absence of minimal residual disease (MRD) predicts longer progression-free survival and overall survival.
items.explanation: The absence of MRD at high sensitivity is associated with longer progression-free and overall survival.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · THERAPY ENDPOINT

items.statement: Pneumococcal polysaccharide vaccines are highly effective in eliciting an antibody response in myeloma patients.
items.explanation: The pneumococcal polysaccharide vaccines may not elicit an antibody response; the conjugate vaccines are more protective.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · SUPPORTIVE THERAPY

items.statement: Routine chronic oral antibiotic prophylaxis is recommended for patients with myeloma.
items.explanation: Routine chronic oral antibiotic prophylaxis is not warranted in these patients.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · SUPPORTIVE THERAPY

items.statement: Renal disease is common in patients with Waldenström's macroglobulinemia.
items.explanation: Renal disease is not common in Waldenström's macroglobulinemia due to little renal excretion of the IgM paraprotein.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · WALDENSTRÖM'S MACROGLOBULINEMIA

items.statement: The presence of MYD88 and CXCR4 mutations affects disease presentation in Waldenström's macroglobulinemia.
items.explanation: MYD88 and CXCR4 mutations influence the clinical presentation, including bone marrow burden and hyperviscosity.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · WALDENSTRÖM'S MACROGLOBULINEMIA

items.statement: The median survival for individuals with Waldenström's macroglobulinemia is approximately 50 months.
items.explanation: The passage states the median survival of affected individuals is ~50 months.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · Waldenström's Macroglobulinemia

items.statement: Rituximab should be used without any precautions in patients with high IgM levels.
items.explanation: Rituximab can produce an IgM flare, so plasmapheresis should be used before rituximab or its use initially withheld.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · Waldenström's Macroglobulinemia

items.statement: Plasmapheresis is a beneficial treatment for POEMS syndrome.
items.explanation: Plasmapheresis does not appear to be of benefit in POEMS syndrome.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · POEMS SYNDROME | HEAVY CHAIN DISEASES

items.statement: Delta and epsilon heavy chain diseases have been reported.
items.explanation: No reports of delta or epsilon heavy chain diseases have appeared.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · POEMS SYNDROME | HEAVY CHAIN DISEASES

items.subtopic: GAMMA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (FRANKLIN'S DISEASE) | ALPHA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (SELIGMANN'S DISEASE) | MU HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE
items.statement: Hyperviscosity is a common problem in Alpha Heavy Chain Disease.
items.explanation: Despite polymerization, hyperviscosity is not a common problem in Alpha Heavy Chain Disease.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · GAMMA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (FRANKLIN'S DISEASE) | ALPHA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (SELIGMANN'S DISEASE) | MU HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE

items.subtopic: GAMMA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (FRANKLIN'S DISEASE) | ALPHA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (SELIGMANN'S DISEASE) | MU HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE
items.statement: IPSID is associated with Campylobacter jejuni.
items.explanation: IPSID, a form of Alpha Heavy Chain Disease, is recognized as an infectious pathogen-associated human lymphoma linked to Campylobacter jejuni.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · GAMMA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (FRANKLIN'S DISEASE) | ALPHA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (SELIGMANN'S DISEASE) | MU HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE

items.assertion: Idiotypes serve as reliable markers for a particular clone of cells.
items.reason: Each antibody is formed and secreted by a single clone with unique structural features in its antigen-binding portion.
items.explanation: The reason correctly explains why idiotypes are reliable markers for a specific clone.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · Plasma Cell Disorders (part 1)

items.assertion: In plasma cell disorders, the control over B lymphocyte maturation is retained.
items.reason: Exposure to antigen normally stimulates proliferation of antibody-secreting cells.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because in plasma cell disorders, the control over this process is lost.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · Plasma Cell Disorders (part 1)

items.assertion: Blacks have nearly twice the incidence of myeloma compared to whites.
items.reason: The higher incidence in blacks may be due to genetic and environmental factors.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not provided as an explanation for the higher incidence.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: The median age at diagnosis of myeloma is 69 years.
items.reason: Myeloma increases in incidence with age.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Patients with myeloma often experience hyponatremia.
items.reason: The M component is cationic, resulting in retention of chloride and decreased anion gap, which leads to pseudohyponatremia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS (part 2) | B

items.assertion: Neurologic symptoms in myeloma patients are primarily due to hyperviscosity.
items.reason: Hypercalcemia can also produce neurologic symptoms such as lethargy and confusion.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but hypercalcemia is not the correct explanation for the primary cause of neurologic symptoms.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS (part 2) | B

items.assertion: Solitary bone plasmacytoma is associated with a median survival of ≥10 years.
items.reason: Both solitary bone and extramedullary plasmacytomas are highly responsive to local radiation therapy.
items.explanation: While the assertion is true, the reason relates more to treatment response than directly explaining the median survival.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · DIAGNOSIS AND STAGING

items.assertion: Serum β₂-microglobulin is a powerful predictor of survival in multiple myeloma.
items.reason: β₂-Microglobulin levels are used to form the basis for the International Staging System (ISS).
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATION

items.assertion: IgG3 myeloma has the highest tendency to form aggregates leading to hyperviscosity.
items.reason: This is due to its genetic determinant of cell proliferation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for the aggregation tendency.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATION

items.assertion: Belantamab mafodotin has unique ophthalmologic toxicity.
items.reason: The drug delivers auristatin, a microtubule inhibitor, to tumor cells.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · RELAPSED DISEASE

items.assertion: Selinexor blocks export of proteins from the cell nucleus.
items.reason: It is a histone deacetylase inhibitor.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false; selinexor is an exportin inhibitor.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · RELAPSED DISEASE

items.assertion: Achieving complete remission (CR) has been a standard goal of therapy for myeloma.
items.reason: Complete remission is defined as the disappearance of serum and urine monoclonal protein with normal bone marrow by light microscopy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why achieving CR has been a standard goal.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · THERAPY ENDPOINT

items.assertion: Prophylactic administration of intravenous γ globulin preparations is used for recurrent serious infections.
items.reason: Patients receiving CAR-T therapy or bispecific antibodies may develop prolonged hypogammaglobulinemia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · SUPPORTIVE THERAPY

items.assertion: Kyphoplasty or vertebroplasty should be considered for patients with painful collapsed vertebra.
items.reason: These procedures directly treat osteonecrosis of the jaw and renal dysfunction.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false because kyphoplasty or vertebroplasty are not treatments for osteonecrosis of the jaw or renal dysfunction.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · SUPPORTIVE THERAPY

items.assertion: Patients with Waldenström's macroglobulinemia often present with peripheral neuropathy.
items.reason: The IgM in some patients may have specificity for myelin-associated glycoprotein (MAG).
items.explanation: Peripheral neuropathy can occur due to anti-MAG antibodies, which are associated with Waldenström's macroglobulinemia.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · WALDENSTRÖM'S MACROGLOBULINEMIA

items.assertion: Bone marrow in Waldenström's macroglobulinemia shows lytic bone lesions.
items.reason: The disease involves the bone marrow with lymphoplasmacytic cell infiltration.
items.explanation: Waldenström's macroglobulinemia does not cause lytic bone lesions, unlike myeloma.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · WALDENSTRÖM'S MACROGLOBULINEMIA

items.subtopic: GAMMA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (FRANKLIN'S DISEASE) | ALPHA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (SELIGMANN'S DISEASE) | MU HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE
items.assertion: Gamma Heavy Chain Disease is frequently associated with autoimmune diseases.
items.reason: It is often seen in patients with rheumatoid arthritis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the association with autoimmune diseases.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · GAMMA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (FRANKLIN'S DISEASE) | ALPHA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (SELIGMANN'S DISEASE) | MU HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE

items.subtopic: GAMMA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (FRANKLIN'S DISEASE) | ALPHA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (SELIGMANN'S DISEASE) | MU HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE
items.assertion: Mu Heavy Chain Disease often presents with respiratory tract involvement.
items.reason: It is characterized by the secretion of isolated mu heavy chains into the serum.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as Mu Heavy Chain Disease rarely involves the respiratory tract, while the reason is true.
items.reference: Plasma Cell Disorders · GAMMA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (FRANKLIN'S DISEASE) | ALPHA HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE (SELIGMANN'S DISEASE) | MU HEAVY CHAIN DISEASE
